inserted.html: Codex CLI is OpenAI's local coding agent for the terminal. Install it globally with npm, then run it from the root of the project you want Codex to inspect or edit.

comment.en: OpenAI's current CLI getting-started docs list npm install -g @openai/codex as the zero-setup install path.
This installs the codex command globally so it is available from any project folder.
Verify that the command is on your PATH and inspect the available subcommands and flags.

rounds.prompt: Sign in and start a first session

inserted.html: Authenticate once, then start Codex from a real project directory. Newer Codex CLI versions expose authentication as the codex login subcommand.

comment.en: The installed CLI in this workspace uses codex login. Some OpenAI help articles mention older login spellings, so checking codex --help is useful.
Follow the browser-based sign-in flow and let Codex store credentials locally.
Starting Codex inside a project gives it the local files and instructions it needs for a useful coding session.

inserted.html: For this tutorial's imported demo, Codex was started in a small folder named demo2 and asked to build a Brownian motion webpage. The next section is the actual imported Codex session trace.
